coredata.dc:title: Durable room-temperature phosphorescence of nitrogen-doped carbon dots-silica composites for Fe3+ detection and anti-counterfeiting
coredata.dc:creator: Hao, Chenxia
Bai, Yunfeng
Zhao, Lu
Bao, Yayan
Bian, Jinai
Xu, Hui
Zhou, Tao
Feng, Feng
coredata.dc:description: Developing durable room temperature phosphorescent carbon dot-based materials with high stability and easy preparation has been a great challenge. Here, through a one-step hydrolysis-assisted carbonization treatment, nitrogen-doped carbon dots encapsulated in silica (N-CDs@silica) are obtained, with outstanding stability and durable room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) lifetime (1.81 s, nearly 9 s to naked eye). Further investigations reveal that CO bonds from N-CDs are the main luminescence centers. The formation of Si–O–C bonds covalently linking N-CDs and silica matrice together within silica sphere, as well as the efficient crosslinking effect within N-CDs, are responsible for RTP emission. Based on the unique optical properties of N-CDs@silica, we have developed the RTP strip probe for selective Fe3+ detection. In addition, we explored N-CDs@silica as security ink for advanced anti-counterfeiting. This work not only provides a strategy to synergistically optimizes the structural and luminescent properties of N-CDs@silica in a simple and efficient manner, but more importantly opens a new perspective for developing simple RTP strip probes for sensor applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Room-temperature phosphorescence
Fe3+ detection
Anti-counterfeiting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of N-CDs@silica-1 to N-CDs@silica-7 and CDs@silica
Preparation of the RTP strip probe
The RTP strip probe for Fe3+
Optical properties and mechanism of N-CDs@silica
The RTP strip probe for Fe3+ detection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Cultivate Scientific Research Excellence Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi
Scientific and Technological Innovation Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Scientific and Technological Innovation Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi ( 2021L368 ), Cultivate Scientific Research Excellence Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi ( 2020KJ023 ) and Shanxi Scholarship Council of China ( 2020–133 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Preparation process of N-CDs@silica. (b) Luminescence properties of N-CDs@silica.

(a) TEM image of N-CDs@silica. (b) Size distribution histogram of N-CDs@silica. (c) HRTEM image of N-CDs@silica. (d) Elemental maps of C, N, O, and Si for N-CDs@silica. (e) XPS spectrum of N-CDs@silica. (f–i) High-resolution XPS spectra and fitting results of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and Si 2p. (i) FTIR spectrum of N-CDs@silica.

(a) Normalized UV–Vis absorption (Abs), fluorescence emission, and excitation spectra of N-CDs@silica. (b) 3D phosphorescence contour map of N-CDs@silica. (c) Phosphorescence emission spectra of N-CDs@silica under various excitation wavelengths. (d) Normalized fluorescence (FL) and phosphorescence (Phos.) emission and excitation spectra of N-CDs@silica. (e) Phosphorescence decay and fitting curves of N-CDs@silica under ambient conditions (λex = 353 nm and λem = 510 nm). (f) Normalized fluorescence (FL) and phosphorescence (Phos.) spectra of N-CDs@silica at low temperature (77 K) under excitation of 350 nm.

(a) TEM and HRTEM (inset) images of CDs-2. (b) XPS spectrum of CDs-2. (c) FTIR spectrum of CDs-2. (d) Fluorescence emission spectra under various excitation wavelengths of CDs-2 solution. (e) Normalized UV–Vis absorption (Abs), fluorescence excitation (Ex), and emission (Em) spectra of CDs-2 solution. (f) FTIR spectra of N-CDs@silica powder and high-temperature treated N-CDs@silica powder.

(a) Photographs of the RTP strip probe with different concentrations of Fe3+ under daylight, UV lamp on and off. (b) Phosphorescence emission spectra of the RTP strip probe with different Fe3+ concentrations (0–4.4 M). (c–d) Linear relationship between P0/P and the concentration of Fe3+. (e) Relative intensity of the RTP strip probe in the presence of various metal ions (4.4 M).

Security protection applications of N-CDs@silica. Green part of the design: N-CDs@silica ink, blue part: interference ink. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Durable room-temperature phosphorescence of nitrogen-doped carbon dots-silica composites for Fe3+ detection and anti-counterfeiting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen, 041004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Material Science

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Shanxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Biosensing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Biosensing, Shanxi Datong University, Datong, 037009, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Chemistry and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen, 041004, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Material Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing durable room temperature phosphorescent carbon dot-based materials with high stability and easy preparation has been a great challenge. Here, through a one-step hydrolysis-assisted carbonization treatment, nitrogen-doped carbon dots encapsulated in silica (N-CDs@silica) are obtained, with outstanding stability and durable room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) lifetime (1.81 s, nearly 9 s to naked eye). Further investigations reveal that CO bonds from N-CDs are the main luminescence centers. The formation of Si–O–C bonds covalently linking N-CDs and silica matrice together within silica sphere, as well as the efficient crosslinking effect within N-CDs, are responsible for RTP emission. Based on the unique optical properties of N-CDs@silica, we have developed the RTP strip probe for selective Fe3+ detection. In addition, we explored N-CDs@silica as security ink for advanced anti-counterfeiting. This work not only provides a strategy to synergistically optimizes the structural and luminescent properties of N-CDs@silica in a simple and efficient manner, but more importantly opens a new perspective for developing simple RTP strip probes for sensor applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Durable room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) has aroused great excitement in recent years due to its long-lived naked-eye recognizable luminescence, large stokes shift and high signal-to-noise ratio, enabling its application in anti-counterfeiting, optoelectronic devices, chemical sensing and bioimaging [1–5]. The current RTP materials are mainly limited to organic compounds and metal coordination complexes [6–8]. However, these materials suffer from disadvantages such as expensive raw materials, complicated preparation and high toxicity, which greatly hinder their further development.

Carbon dots (CDs), as an up-and-coming carbon-based luminescent nanomaterials, possess the merits of low cost, abundant surface functional groups, low toxicity, high stability and excellent biocompatibility, which endow them distinct benefits for anti-counterfeiting, optoelectronic devices, sensing and bioimaging [9–13]. Generally, CDs exhibit intense fluorescence, but when CDs are immobilized in a certain matrix, such as potassium aluminum sulfate [14], recrystallized urea/biuret [15], NaCl hybrid crystals [16], silica gel [17,18], or self-protection matrix based on self-crosslinking of CDs [19–22], significant phosphorescence can be detected. In this method, embedding CDs in a solid matrix minimizes the dissipation of triplet excitons through nonradiative transitions by constructing a rigid environment, and thus promotes RTP generation. However, previous studies were mainly based on a multistep synthesis method where CDs were firstly synthesized and then post-processed for introduction into the matrix, which increased the complexity of experiment and screening time. Also, most of them exhibit relatively short luminescence lifetime and poor stability, making them unsuitable for persistent luminescence applications. Compared to these matrices, silica matrix is chemically stable and facile to obtain, and they can provide more effective confined shielding through a three-dimensional network of Si–O links.

In addition, heteroatoms doping (N, O, S, P, etc.) facilitates spin-orbit coupling, thus promoting the intersystem crossing from the lowest excited singlet state to the triplet state [23–26]. For instance, Zhao's group embedded CDs into poly(vinyl alcohol) matrix with luminescence lifetime of 380 ms [25]. The CO bonds on the surfaces of CDs were shown to be the origin of RTP due to its strong spin-orbit coupling of aromatic carbonyls. Zhou et al. incorporated N-CDs into melting recrystallization urea and biuret to obtain RTP composite with luminescence lifetime of 1.06 s [26]. The CN bonds on the surface of N-CDs were found to be induced RTP. Besides, heteroatoms doping helps to produce abundant surface states on CDs, which will facilitate the broadening of applications in ion analysis [27–29]. More importantly, nitrogen doping has also been reported to provide more crosslinking points to fix and restrict the vibration and rotation of the luminescence centers, improving the performance of RTP [19,30].

Herein, a rational strategy has been constructed to achieve nitrogen-doped carbon dots@silica composites (N-CDs@silica) with long-afterglow RTP by one-step hydrothermal treatment of triethylenetetramine and tetraethoxysilane (TEOS). In the hydrothermal process, N-CDs and silica are formed simultaneously, during which silica as a host matrix adheres and captures N-CDs, forming the composites of N-CDs encapsulated in silica. Our results reveal that CO bonds from N-CDs are the main luminescence centers. The formation of Si–O–C bonds linking N-CDs and silica matrices together within silica sphere, as well as the efficient crosslinking effect within N-CDs, are responsible for RTP emission. In addition, the N-CDs@silica exhibit excellent stability and RTP lifetime of 1.81 s (nearly 9 s to naked eye), and were successfully developed as a solid-state RTP strip probe for visual and quantitative detection of Fe3+, and as optical ink for advanced anti-counterfeiting.

Tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), triethylenetetramine, diethylenetriamine, tetraethyenepentamine and polyethylenimine were acquired from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Ethanol, citric acid, ethylenediamine, propylamine, diethylamine, triethylamine and polyvinyl alcohol were provided by Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China).

The morphologies of the products were described by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100F) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi SU8020). The particle size distribution measurement was conducted with a Zeta potential & Dynamic light scattering analyzer (DLS, DelsaMax PRO). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were determined by a Rigaku SmartLab SE diffractometer. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were measured using a WQF520 FTIR spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were taken in a K-Alpha spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific). Raman spectra were done on confocal Raman spectrometer (Renishaw inVia) with an excitation wavelength of 514 nm laser. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a PerkinElmer Lambda-35 spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence spectra, absolute quantum efficiency, and decay curves were performed using a HORIBA QM 8000 spectrofluorometer.

First, TEOS (4.5 mL) and triethylenetetramine (1.1 mL) were added in ultrapure water (20 mL), followed by transferring to a poly(p-phenylene) reactor and heating at 240 °C for 10 h. After cooling, the unreacted small molecules were eliminated by centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 5 min, and the sediment deposited at the bottom of the centrifuge tube was washed several times with ethanol. Finally, the N-CDs@silica can be obtained as a white powder by freeze-drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N-CDs@silica-1 to N-CDs@silica-7 and CDs@silica
ce:section.ce:para: N-CDs@silica-x were acquired by the same procedure as N-CDs@silica except that triethylenetetramine was replaced by different types of amines (diethylamine, triethylamine, propylamine, ethylenediamine, diethylenetriamine, tetraethyenepentamine and polyethylenimine) with the same nitrogen content to obtain N-CDs@silica-1 to N-CDs@silica-7, respectively. The sample with no amine added was labeled as CDs@silica.

CDs-2 were acquired by the same procedure as CDs@silica except that TEOS was replaced by ethanol. After cooling, the obtained solution was dialyzed in dialysis bag (MWCO: 1000 Da) against ultrapure water for 10 h to remove small molecules. The final product was freeze-dried.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the RTP strip probe
ce:section.ce:para: N-CDs@silica (0.25 g) were dispersed in ultrapure water (5 mL) and mixed thoroughly. The N-CDs@silica solution (50 μL) was then dropped onto filter paper by drop-coating method, followed by heating the filter paper at 60 °C for 20 min. The RTP strip probe was used for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The RTP strip probe for Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: Different concentrations of Fe3+ (10 μL) were dropped onto the RTP strip probe and then the phosphorescence intensity was measured at room temperature. The selectivity of the RTP strip probe for Fe3+ was compared with various metal ions such as Ni2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Hg2+, Zn2+, K+, Na+, Cu2+ and Ag+ under identical conditions.

A typical formulation: 50 mg of the fine powder was firstly mixed with 300 μL ultrapure water, and then diluted with 1.5 mL of polyvinyl alcohol solution (1.0 g in 15 mL water). After mixing thoroughly by sonication, the as-obtained suspension can be employed as security printing ink.

Blue fluorescent CDs were readily synthesized according to a previously reported method [31]. Ethylenediamine (335 μL) and citric acid (1.051 g) were mixed uniformly in ultrapure water (10 mL), followed by transferring to a poly(p-phenylene) reactor and heating at 200 °C for 5 h. The obtained CDs were directly employed as interference ink.

Preparation of N-CDs@silica and their luminescence properties are shown in Fig. 1 a. In this system, the N-CDs@silica were achieved through the hydrothermal reaction of TEOS and triethylenetetramine. First, TEOS was hydrolyzed rapidly to silicic acid and ethanol, under the catalysis of triethylenetetramine as a base. For silicic acid, it underwent a polycondensation reaction to form silica spheres. At the same time, ethanol together with triethylenetetramine were reacted to generate N-CDs through sequential oxidation, dehydration and crosslinking polymerization. Accompanying what happened is that N-CDs were encapsulated into silica sphere matrix, leading to the formation of N-CDs@silica composites. As depicted in Fig. 1b, the resultant N-CDs@silica exhibit blue fluorescence under UV irradiation. Especially, a longer duration of yellow green RTP (lasting for nearly 9 s to naked eye) can be observed at ambient conditions after ceasing UV irradiation.

SEM (Fig. S1) and TEM (Fig. 2 a) images reveal spherical morphology of N-CDs@silica, with a clean and smooth surface. The histogram of size distribution measured by DLS (Fig. 2b) shows that the average diameter is approximately 720 nm. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 2c) provides a sharp zoom-in profile of the particles. No distinct lattice fringes are found, demonstrating amorphous structure [32–34]. In addition, the silica spheres themselves do not emit light, while the composites can emit phosphorescence, from which the presence of polymer carbon dots can be inferred [33,34]. This is further confirmed by Raman spectra (Fig. S2a) and XRD pattern (Fig. S2b) that there are no relevant characteristic peaks of the crystal lattice [31,33,35]. Although it is too difficult to directly observe silica-confined N-CDs by TEM because of the small size and low mass density for N-CDs, the carbon and nitrogen from N-CDs are evident in the interior of silica from elemental mapping images (Fig. 2d), demonstrating the successful confinement of N-CDs.

The chemical composition and surface structure of N-CDs@silica were characterized using XPS and FTIR. For the XPS spectrum (Fig. 2e), obvious peaks are observed at around 105.8 eV (Si 2p), 287.7 eV (C 1s), 402.1 eV (N 1s) and 535.0 eV (O 1s), indicating the existence of Si, O, C and N elements in N-CDs@silica. In the high-resolution XPS, the C 1s band (Fig. 2f) displays three contributions of CC/C–C at 285.3 eV, C–O/C–N at 286.6 eV, and CO at 287.8 eV [17]. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2g) is fitted with two contributions at 399.2 eV for N–H and at 402.2 eV for C–N [36]. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 2h) shows three contributions at 532.3 eV for CO, 533.4 eV for C–O and 533.8 eV for Si–O bonds, respectively [37]. The Si 2p region (Fig. 2i) contains two contributions at 104.0 and 106.0 eV, corresponding to Si–O–C and Si–Ox, respectively [18]. Similarly, CO at 1643 cm−1, Si–O–C centered at 1238 cm−1 and Si–O at 478 cm−1 in N-CDs@silica are also suggested by FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2j) [38–40]. Besides, the stretching vibration of O–H/N–H appears at around 3425 cm−1 [40]. The characteristic vibrations at 1114 and 800 cm−1 are attributed to asymmetrical and symmetrical stretching vibration of Si–O–Si [41]. The results of XPS and FTIR demonstrate that N-CDs are covalently connected to the silica matrix by Si–O–C bonds, which may bring enhanced crosslinking effect to effectively stabilize triplet excitons by restraining the nonradiative transition, consequently triggering RTP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and mechanism of N-CDs@silica
ce:section.ce:para: The optical behaviors of N-CDs@silica were studied. As shown in absorption spectrum (Fig. 3 a), two typical characteristic absorption peaks at 286 and 351 nm are observed, derived from π–π* transition of CC bonds and n–π* transition of CO bonds, respectively [17,42]. The fluorescence emission of N-CDs@silica at 453 nm can be excited in the range of 300–420 nm, which overlaps with the absorption of CO bands, thus indicating that CO bonds are responsible for the fluorescence emission [22]. In addition, the corresponding fluorescence emission profiles of N-CDs@silica under various excitation wavelengths are shown in Fig. S3, it reveals characteristic excitation-dependent fluorescence emission of N-CDs@silica with an optimal excitation at 345 nm. Further, the fluorescence decay curve (Fig. S4 and Table S1) is fitted by a biexponential function with lifetime components of 9.07 ns (37.81%) and 3.75 ns (62.19%). The average lifetime is calculated to be 6.92 ns. The excitation-dependent luminescence and decay features suggest the existence of multiple emissive channels in N-CDs@silica, consistent with many previous reports [22,43]. The absolute fluorescence quantum yield measured at 345 nm excitation is around 8.42%.

After ceasing UV irradiation, N-CDs@silica exhibit distinct yellow green RTP at ambient conditions. Fig. 3b shows 3D phosphorescence contour map of N-CDs@silica, where a narrow emission centered at around 510 nm is observed. The RTP emission position remains nearly unchanged as the excitation wavelengths ranging from 280 to 400 nm (optimal excitation at 353 nm) (Fig. 3c). Compared with fluorescence spectrum, the RTP emission shows an obvious wavelength red-shift. Furthermore, similar fluorescence and RTP excitation spectra indicate that the RTP originate from the same chemical structure as fluorescence, i.e. the subfluorescent CO groups (Fig. 3d) [22,27,42]. RTP decay curve is measured in Fig. 3e. The N-CDs@silica exhibit triexponential decay with lifetime components of 0.20 s (2.17%), 1.36 s (67.32%) and 2.39 s (30.51%) (Table S2), indicating the presence of multiple delay channels, which may derive from various chemical environments of CO groups [22,44]. Especially, the average lifetime of N-CDs@silica is calculated to be 1.81 s, which is a relatively long value compared with that published for CD-based RTP materials, offering great potential for sensing and anti-counterfeiting applications (Table S3). For deeper understanding RTP property of N-CDs@silica, the low temperature (77 K) fluorescence and RTP emission spectra were measured to evaluate the energy gap between lowest excited triplet (T1) and singlet state (S1) (Fig. 3f). The small energy gap of 0.28 eV (S1, 2.73 eV; T1, 2.45 eV) makes the intersystem crossing process from the S1 to T1 possible, thus facilitating the generation of RTP phenomenon [45,46]. The absolute RTP quantum yield is about 6.0% under the excitation of 350 nm. In addition, N-CDs@silica show excellent stability, including photostability, storage and thermal stability (Figs. S5 and S6), which makes N-CDs@silica very attractive for practical applications.

To validate the luminescence mechanism, two groups of control experiments were carried out. In the first set of control experiment, CDs-2 were prepared by replacing TEOS with ethanol and other experimental conditions remained unchanged. TEM image of CDs-2 reveals a uniform dispersion with an average size of around 10 nm (Fig. 4 a). HRTEM image shows that most of CDs-2 are amorphous structure without obvious lattice fringes, in line with N-CDs@silica. The chemical environment and functional groups of CDs-2 were investigated by XPS and FTIR. The XPS full spectrum (Fig. 4b) of CDs-2 contains C (285 eV), N (399 eV), and O (531 eV) elements. The high-resolution spectrum of C 1s (Fig. S7a) displays three peaks at 285.0, 286.3, and 288.8 eV for C–C/CC, C–O/C–N, and CO/CN, respectively. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. S7b) displays two peaks at 399.2 and 401.3 eV, corresponding to N–H and C–N, respectively. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. S7c) contains 533.5 and 531.6 eV peaks attributed to C–O and CO bonds. FTIR spectrum of CDs-2 (Fig. 4c) shows stretching vibrations of O–H/N–H at 3277 cm−1, CO/CN at 1574 cm−1, CC at 1458 cm−1, C–N at 1306 cm−1 and C–O at 1118 cm−1. The XPS and FTIR results show that the CDs-2 are enriched with functional groups that can provide binding sites for the formation of crosslinking network with silica matrix. Notice that CDs-2 exhibit blue fluorescence but no phosphorescence. It is reasonable to figure out that the matrix of silica network is extremely important for achieving RTP. Fig. 4d demonstrates that CDs-2 have similar excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior as N-CDs@silica. The fluorescence decay curve of CDs-2 is fitted by a biexponential function with lifetime components of 7.18 ns (34.15%) and 2.77 ns (65.85%), and the average lifetime is calculated to be 5.30 ns, similar to that of N-CDs@silica (Fig. S8 and Table S4). The absorption spectrum of CDs-2 displays two peaks at about 289 and 316 nm, identified as π–π* transition of CC and n–π* transition of CO, respectively (Fig. 4e). The wavelength position of CO overlaps highly the maximum excitation at 370 nm of CDs-2. These data highlight again that the luminescence center of N-CDs@silica originates from the CO groups of N-CDs. The second set of control experiment was designed by high-temperature treatment of the N-CDs@silica at 900 °C for 24 h. As expected, the high-temperature treated N-CDs@silica did not exhibit any emission. The FTIR spectral characterization (Fig. 4f) stands out the absence of luminescence centers (CO) as responsible for this.

On the basis of above results, the photoluminescence mechanism of N-CDs@silica could be proposed as follows (Fig. S9). N-CDs with abundant subfluorophores (CO) are the main luminescence centers, and meanwhile are also widely recognized to be responsible for the origin of phosphorescence. These groups facilitate n–π* transition and thus promote the spin-forbidden intersystem crossing of S1 to T1 to populate triplet excitons. The silica network as a confined matrix is beneficial for restricting vibration or rotation of subﬂuorophores in N-CDs, which largely suppresses the nonradiative transition to effectively stabilize the triplet excitons. Besides, the covalently connected Si–O–C bonds between N-CDs and silica further enhances the rigidity of the assembly and protects luminescence centers, jointly promoting RTP emission.

Amines act as catalysts and dopants in this synthetic strategy, it is necessary to discuss their role more thoroughly (Table S5). CDs@silica show weak phosphorescence, indicating that the ethanol produced by hydrolysis alone can also be cross-linked, but not as tightly as the amine addition. For N-CDs@silica-1 and N-CDs@silica-2, diethylamines and triethylamines are types of mono-secondary and mono-tertiary amines that do not provide effective crosslinking points to fix and restrict the vibration and rotation of the luminescence centers, and have a negative effect. In comparison with N-CDs@silica-3 and N-CDs@silica 4–7, it can be concluded that mono-primary amine (N-CDs@silica-3) has a single functional group, while polyamines (N-CDs@silica 4–7) have multifunctional groups allowing for denser crosslinking. For N-CDs@silica-6, the longer chain in tetraethyenepentamine allows greater freedom of vibration and rotation, thus weakening the RTP performance. Likewise, abundant branched chains of polyethylenimine produce large steric hindrance for crosslinking reaction (N-CDs@silica-7). While for N-CDs@silica-4, N-CDs@silica-5 and N-CDs@silica, the reason may be that triethylenetetramine gives a stronger crosslinking ability. Therefore, efficient crosslinking inside N-CDs plays an important role in RTP emission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The RTP strip probe for Fe3+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the emission properties of N-CDs@silica and the abundant groups on its surface, it is supposed that the N-CDs@silica can be used to develop optical sensors that interact with common metal cations. To promote and expand the application, the RTP strip probe was fabricated by coating N-CDs@silica on common filter paper. Common filter paper, like most substances including urine and serum, has a distinct fluorescent background, yet no RTP background is observed. Therefore, detection of real samples with the RTP strip probe can avoid complex sample pretreatments such as deproteinization and centrifugation required for analysis of biological fluids by spectrofluorometry [47,48].

Hereby, we investigated the phosphorescence response of various metal cations to the RTP strip probe. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the RTP strip probe is extremely selective for Fe3+. The emission intensity of the RTP strip probe gradually decreases with increasing Fe3+ concentration (Fig. 5b). The linear dependence of quenching efficiency (P0/P) on Fe3+ concentration (C) is presented in the range of 0–0.13 M and 0.13–4.4 M (Fig. 5c and d). The linear regression equations are P0/P = 1.002 + 5.412C (R2 = 0.994) and P0/P = 1.618 + 0.274C (R2 = 0.990), respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) evaluated by 3σ/k (k is the slope of the linear curve and σ is the standard deviation of 10 blank samples) is 3.4 mM, which is below the maximum tolerance (5.4 mM) allowed in drinking water by the United States Environmental Protection Agency [49]. In contrast, other metal ions such as Ni2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Hg2+, Zn2+, K+, Na+, Cu2+ and Ag+ have little effect on the RTP strip probe (Figs. 5e and S10). The effect of coexisting ions on the quenching efficiency of the RTP strip probe by Fe3+ was further investigated and no significant effect was observed (Fig. S11). These results indicate that the RTP strip probe has good sensing performance for Fe3+. In addition, we find that the fluorescence of the RTP strip probe is also selectively quenched by Fe3+ (Fig. 5a). The simultaneous quenching of fluorescence and phosphorescence by Fe3+ is not a coincidence. The fluorescence lifetime and phosphorescence lifetime are used to evaluate the quenching mechanism. The fluorescence lifetimes are almost unchanged before and after the addition of Fe3+ (Fig. S12a and Table S6), as are the RTP lifetimes (Fig. S12b and Table S7). This may result from the formation of a nonfluorescent complex between N-CDs@silica and Fe3+, which induces partial electron transfer from the triplet state to the Fe3+ orbital through nonradiative electron transfer, disrupting the energy level structure of N-CDs@silica caused mainly by the CO groups on the surface, and thus RTP quenching occurs. In addition, inner filter effect may also have a weak contribution.

The stability and reproducibility of the RTP strip probe were also investigated. As shown in Fig. S13a, the phosphorescence intensity of the RTP strip probe has no significant change after 1 h of continuous UV irradiation, indicating its good stability. Fig. S13b shows the response of 10 RTP strip probes in the absence and presence of Fe3+ with a small RSD of 1.7%, suggesting its good reproducibility. Futhermore, the RTP strip probe is applied to the determination of Fe3+ in lake water and serum samples. Lake water is taken from Wenying Lake (Datong, Shanxi Province) and filtered through 0.22 mm membrane before use. The samples do not require any pretreatment except for dilution. The recoveries of Fe3+ detection are 93.5–106% for lake water samples and 95.0–103% for serum samples (Table S8), revealing the reliability of the RTP strip probe in real samples.

Given the outstanding feature, we show a primary application as security ink for information encryption and anti-counterfeiting. As shown in Fig. 6 , the pattern ‘88888’ and the logo of a lotus flower were printed on nonfluorescent filter paper. The green part was marked with the security ink, and the blue section was printed by interference ink having similar fluorescence property as that of security ink. All patterns were nearly invisible under daylight after complete drying. When exposed under UV irradiation, wrong information with the blue ‘88888’ was observed. Outstanding contrast, after ceasing the UV lamp, the pattern turned yellow green and the correct code ‘35079’ appeared. Similarly, the lotus flower pattern presented blue fluorescence upon UV irradiation. Once stopping the excitation, the part of flower disappeared immediately, and the stem and leave exhibited yellow green. Compared to traditional luminescent materials that only exhibit fluorescence signal, the N-CDs@silica with ultralong RTP lifetime can provide more capabilities to combat counterfeiting.

In summary, a novel CDs-based RTP material has been prepared through a facile hydrolysis-assisted carbonization design, instead of the classic multistep synthesis approach. Significantly, the N-CDs@silica display strong fluorescence (maximum emission at 450 nm) and RTP (maximum emission at 510 nm) with lifetime of 1.81 s under ambient conditions. Further investigations reveal that the N-CDs inside the N-CDs@silica are the main luminescence centers. The silica network as the blocking matrix plays a critical role in the production of RTP by effectively restricting vibration and rotation of the luminescence centers. Moreover, the formation of Si–O–C bonds between N-CDs and silica matrix, as well as the efficient crosslinking effect inside N-CDs, are also deemed to favor RTP emission. Based on these properties, N-CDs@silica can be used as a solid-state RTP strip probe for the visualization and quantification of Fe3+, and it is easier to carry and store compared to solution sensors. In addition, this RTP strip probe is easy to prepare, inexpensive, environmentally friendly, and has good stability under environmental conditions, making it suitable as a probe for Fe3+ detection. Finally, we explored CDs@silica as an optical ink for advanced anti-counterfeiting.

Chenxia Hao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Yunfeng Bai: Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Project administration. Lu Zhao: Investigation. Yayan Bao: Investigation. Jinai Bian: Investigation. Hui Xu: Investigation. Tao Zhou: Investigation, Validation. Feng Feng: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to inﬂuence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Scientific and Technological Innovation Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (2021L368), Cultivate Scientific Research Excellence Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (2020KJ023) and Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (2020–133).
